journal_entry.description: Discounting of 90-day non-interest-bearing note receivable at bank, 15% annual discount rate, 40 days remaining
journal_entry.rationale: Ford discounted a $100,000 note receivable with 40 days remaining at 15% annual rate. Discount charge = $100,000 × 15% × (40/365) = $1,643.84. Net proceeds = $100,000 - $1,643.84 = $98,356.16. Since management determined this qualifies as derecognition under IFRS 9 (transfer of risks and rewards), the note receivable is removed from the books. The discount charge represents the financing cost/loss on the transaction and is recorded as an expense. Cash increases by net proceeds (asset increase), financing expense increases (expense increase), and notes receivable decreases (asset decrease). This matches the tuple structure: 1 asset increase (Cash), 1 expense increase (Financing Expense), and 1 asset decrease (Notes Receivable). No tax applies to financial instrument transactions.

output.reason: Ford discounts the note at the bank, receiving cash proceeds. This creates two debit lines: (1) Cash received = asset increase (even though net of discount charges), and (2) Loss on discounting = expense increase. The discount charges represent the financing cost/loss from selling the note before maturity at a discount. The note receivable is derecognized (credited), but we only classify the debit side here.

debit_classifier.reason: Ford discounts the note at the bank, receiving cash proceeds. This creates two debit lines: (1) Cash received = asset increase (even though net of discount charges), and (2) Loss on discounting = expense increase. The discount charges represent the financing cost/loss from selling the note before maturity at a discount. The note receivable is derecognized (credited), but we only classify the debit side here.

output.reason:
Ford discounts (sells) the note receivable to the bank and receives cash. Since this qualifies as derecognition under IFRS 9, Ford removes the note receivable from its books. The credit side consists of:
1. Note Receivable decrease (asset decrease) - removing the $100,000 note from books
2. Interest/Discount Expense decrease (expense decrease) - the bank's discount charge reduces the cash received, creating a financing cost that is debited, so crediting expense represents the offset/recognition of this cost component

Actually, reconsidering: When Ford discounts the note, the credits are:
1. Notes Receivable (asset decrease) - the note is removed from Ford's books at its carrying value
The discount charge is a loss/expense on Ford's side (debited), not credited. The cash received is debited.

So the credit side is simply: Notes Receivable removed = asset decrease (1 line)

So the credit side is simply: Notes Receivable removed = asset decrease (1 line)

output.reason: Entry correctly records the discounting of a note receivable with derecognition under IFRS 9. Balance verified: debits ($98,356.16 + $1,643.84 = $100,000) equal credits ($100,000). Calculation correct: 40 days remaining (90-50), discount = $100,000 × 15% × (40/365) = $1,643.84, net proceeds = $98,356.16. Accounts appropriate: Cash debit for proceeds received, Financing Expense debit for discount cost, Notes Receivable credit for derecognition. Directionality correct for all account types. No tax applies to financial instrument transactions. All elements verified.
